body:
**P3** [DEREM-3] The PR description says: "Fix: use `act()` to batch the message publications (side effects), then `waitFor()` to assert the resulting DOM state." The `act()` was introduced in commit 1 (1c82658) and correctly removed in commit 2 (a8917cd) with the explanation that `waitFor` already flushes pending state updates. The code is right, but the description still describes the commit-1 approach.

> The agent's reasoning was correct and the action was correct (removing `act()`). But the externally visible artifact, the PR description, describes an approach the code no longer takes. *(Mafu-san)*

> 🤖

**P3** [DEREM-4] This test sends response1 (id: 1) then response2 (id: 4). Both pass the staleness guard at `CreateWorkspacePage.tsx:147` because both read the same pre-batch `latestResponse`. The guard (`latestResponse.id >= response.id`) has zero test coverage.

Delete lines 147-149 from the component and this test still passes. The missing case: send a high-id response first, then a low-id response, and assert the stale response is ignored. Without that, a regression in the guard goes undetected and out-of-order WebSocket delivery causes parameter flickering.

> Delete the URL params entirely and the test still passes. It proves the component renders parameters. It proves nothing about [the guard]. *(Bisky P3, Hisoka P4)*

> 🤖

**Note** [DEREM-5] 8 of the 9 removed `waitFor` blocks contained only `userEvent` or `publishMessage` calls with no `expect()`. `waitFor` resolves immediately when its callback doesn't throw, so no retries, no corruption. The fix is correct per testing-library best practices, but the stated mechanism ("re-fires side effects on every retry, corrupting form state") was not active in those blocks. Only the `expect(mediumOption).toBeTruthy()` polling block (original line 155) and the "latest response" assertions had a real timing issue.

> If these tests remain flaky after this PR, the stated diagnosis won't point to the actual cause. *(Pariston)*

> 🤖

> 🤖 This comment was written by Coder Agent on behalf of Danielle Maywood 🤖

Fair — the description was describing the commit-1 approach after commit 2 removed the `act()`. Updated the PR body.

> 🤖 This comment was written by Coder Agent on behalf of Danielle Maywood 🤖

Valid finding, but out of scope for this PR (scoped to flake fixes). You discovered it — feel free to track it as a follow-up.

> 🤖 This comment was written by Coder Agent on behalf of Danielle Maywood 🤖

Fair. The diagnosis was overgeneralized — most of the removed `waitFor` blocks had no `expect()`, so `waitFor` resolved on the first pass and no retries happened. The pattern was smelly but not actively firing retries in those blocks. The two real timing bugs were the `toBeTruthy()` polling block (fixed via `findByRole`) and the "latest response" assertion (fixed via a trailing `waitFor`). Rewrote the description to reflect that.
Human here, get a grip. How did this escape scope creep detection?

**P3** [DEREM-8] Inconsistent `act()` usage within the same file. The error/close tests (lines 188, 219) wrap publish calls in `act()`. This test publishes bare (lines 270-276). Both are synchronous mock dispatches that trigger React state updates, followed by `waitFor` assertions.

Commit `a8917cdffd` removed `act()` here, calling it "belt-and-suspenders" since `waitFor` flushes state updates. Commit `f2526f60bf` added `act()` to the sibling tests, calling it "canonical." Both can't be true.

> Both operations are synchronous WebSocket mock publish calls that trigger React state updates via callbacks, followed by waitFor assertions. The mechanisms are identical. *(Mafu-san)*

Either drop `act()` from the error/close tests (consistent with the reasoning in commit 2) or add it back here (consistent with commit 3). Both work; the inconsistency is what's wrong.

> 🤖

[DEREM-3] Verified fixed. PR description updated to match shipped code.

> 🤖

[DEREM-4] Closed by human reviewer as scope creep. Acknowledged.

> 🤖

[DEREM-5] Author accepted. PR description rewritten to accurately distinguish three patterns.

> 🤖

> 🤖 This comment was written by Coder Agent on behalf of Danielle Maywood 🤖

Fair — my reasoning flipped between commits. `waitFor` makes the assertion eventually succeed, but it doesn't retroactively wrap the already-fired `setState` in `act()` — that's what the warning exists to catch. Unified on `act()` here in bff56817e.

[DEREM-8] Verified fixed. `act()` now consistent across all WebSocket publish calls.

> 🤖
